channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: U.S.-Iran Talks May Continue, but the Cease-Fire Is Over
item.description: Regional powers push Washington and Tehran to pause strikes to allow for negotiations.

item.title: Evacuating Art From Ukraine’s Front Lines
item.description: The Lithuanian curators working to protect paintings from Putin.

item.title: The CIA’s First Failed Coup
item.description: Kim Philby, parachute drops, and what really happened in communist Albania.

item.title: Surviving an Oil Spill in Louisiana, and More New Fiction for July
item.description: Stephanie Soileau’s ‘Should the Waters Take Us’ depicts a Cajun community in Louisiana.

item.title: A World of Scams
item.description: ‘The Yahoo Boys’ shows how con artists can be victims too.

item.title: Are Movies to Blame?
item.description: A renowned film historian asks if moving pictures led us to something rotten.

item.title: How Ukraine Figured Out Trump World
item.description: Former Ukrainian Foreign Minister Dmytro Kuleba on the White House’s seeming change in tone toward Kyiv.

item.title: What in the World?
item.description: Test yourself on the week of July 4: China test-fires a missile, the Philippines begins an impeachment trial, and Turkey hosts the NATO leaders’ summit.

item.title: How to train for a heatwave
item.description: A nice, hot bath can go a long way

item.title: Cover Story newsletter: The man who would change Russia
item.description: An exclusive look at how we designed our cover

item.title: The Israel-Lebanon Recipe for Never-Ending War
item.description: Lebanon’s political circumstances, together with Iran’s strengthened regional position, make Hezbollah’s complete disarmament highly unlikely. By conditioning Israel’s withdrawal from Lebanon on this outcome—but with no provisions about cutting Hezbollah’s Iranian lifeline—the new framework agreement is dead on arrival.

item.title: Israel’s Pivot to Asia
item.description: Israel's reliance on longstanding partnerships with the United States and a handful of European countries is no longer sufficient. But can it build durable relationships in Asia?

item.title: BNP’s First 100 Days Raise Troubling National Questions
item.description: Political exclusion, economic uncertainty, security concerns, and diplomatic imbalance mark Prime Minister Tarique Rahman’s fledgling regime.

item.title: Why This Energy Shock Is Different
item.description: Unlike recent energy crises, today’s geopolitical shock is destroying supply rather than rerouting it, exposing the limits of the standard policy toolkit. Governments can mitigate its impact on those most exposed, but only if fiscal and monetary policy work in concert.

item.title: Vietnam’s Economic Statecraft in the Global Chip Race
item.description: Hanoi is ramping up semiconductor investment, but will it succeed in boosting domestic capabilities?

item.title: Why Russia May Share Intelligence – Not Satellites – With North Korea
item.description: Rather than helping North Korea build military satellites, what if Moscow simply shares the intelligence collected by its existing satellite network?

item.title: Is Ukraine’s Decentralized Drone Innovation a Blip or a Revolution?
item.description: Weapons reworked on the front are driving Kyiv’s recent successes.

item.title: Why Kazakhstan Is Changing Where Its History Begins
item.description: Under Tokayev, Kazakhstan is reshaping its official understanding of statehood, extending the country's historical narrative centuries beyond independence

item.title: Modi in Melbourne: Wide-ranging Australia-India Cooperation Deepens
item.description: The contemporary Australia-India relationship is built on an increasingly interconnected web of institutional cooperation.

item.title: Tibet’s Role in China’s Green Energy Supply Chain
item.description: Tibet stands at the forefront of China’s wind, solar, and hydropower. But the Tibetan people have no say over how their resources are used.

item.title: What the World Cup Meant to Mexico
item.description: The country is done hosting the tournament, but it will remember the “collective joy.”

item.title: China’s Erasure of Tibet Is Accelerating
item.description: In recent years, the international community has stood idly by while China has systematically eradicated Tibetan culture and identity. With the authorities stepping up their assimilation efforts, Asia risks not only losing one of its oldest civilizations, but also facing a China that is bigger, bolder, and more powerful than ever.

item.title: Where is the home of football?
item.description: England invented the sport. But its modern centre of gravity lies elsewhere

item.title: Why Netanyahu Is Crying Wolf on Turkey
item.description: Election politics give the Israeli leader an incentive to inflate the threat.

item.title: Judging the Enhanced Games
item.description: If fairness in sports required a level playing field that all who wish to compete could afford, that would be a reason for banning expensive performance-enhancing drugs. But it would be a reason for banning expensive high-tech swimming suits and expensive high-tech running shoes, too.

item.title: What Is the Digital-Money Debate Really About?
item.description: Both the US and Europe are moving to create digital money, but neither has the right idea about how to do it. The key is to allow “narrow banks,” which issue money but not credit, to keep their reserves in central banks and provide capped interest payments to coin holders.

item.title: The Power Struggle at the Heart of Nepal’s Ruling Party
item.description: Differences between Prime Minister Balendra Shah and RSP chief Rabi Lamichhane, two politicians with competing centers of authority, were inevitable.

item.title: Thailand Needs to Soak Its Own Rich, Not Just Wealthy Tourists
item.description: While the government focuses on burnishing its appeal to well-heeled tourists, massive holdings of Thai elite wealth remain effectively untaxed.

item.title: Philippine Defense Chief Dismisses Chinese Scholars’ Claim Over Northernmost Province
item.description: The scholars described the Batanes Islands as a “natural geographical extension” of Taiwan, which therefore belonged to China.

item.title: Why We Know More About China’s Next-Generation Fighters Than America’s
item.description: As internet sleuthing replaces traditional intelligence collection, one big mystery about the PLA remains.

item.title: Iran Is Losing Iraq
item.description: Baghdad goes its own way.

item.title: How to Win the Defense Innovation Contest
item.description: America and its allies must pool their efforts.

item.title: Is AI Truly Transforming Work in the Asia-Pacific?
item.description: While AI use is proliferating, it is unclear whether it is moving into the areas of the economy where most of the region’s 2 billion workers are actually employed.

item.title: ASEAN Foreign Ministers to Meet Myanmar Foreign Minister This Weekend
item.description: Tin Maung Swe is likely to press the case for diplomatic normalization between ASEAN and the military-backed government in Naypyidaw.

item.title: India and Sri Lanka’s Deep Sea Mining Deadlock
item.description: Regional geopolitical tensions and a legal stalemate are constraining both countries' cobalt ambitions in the Afanasy Nikitin Seamount.

item.title: How dementia is being defeated
item.description: Incidence among the elderly is falling fast
